subject: Physics
q: Least count of a vernier caliper is $\frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of one division on the main scale is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the number of divisions of main scale that coincide with $\mathrm{N}$ divisions of vernier scale is :
question: Least count of a vernier caliper is $\frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of one division on the main scale is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. Then the number of divisions of main scale that coincide with $\mathrm{N}$ divisions of vernier scale is :
solution:
Least count of vernier calipers $=\frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\because$ Least count $=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD}$
let $\mathrm{x}$ no. of divisions of main scale coincides with $\mathrm{N}$ division of vernier scale, then
$\begin{aligned} & 1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{\mathrm{x} \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}}{\mathrm{~N}} \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{20 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~cm}=1 \mathrm{~mm}-\frac{\mathrm{x} \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}}{\mathrm{~N}} \\ & \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~N}} \mathrm{~mm}=1 \mathrm{~mm}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{N}} \mathrm{mm} \\ & \mathrm{x}=\left(1-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~N}}\right) \mathrm{N} \\ & \mathrm{x}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~N}-1}{2}\end{aligned}$

question:
In a meter bridge experiment, the circuit diagram and the corresponding observation table are shown in figure.
S. No. RΩ lcm

1.
2.
3.
4.
1000
100
10
1
60
13
1.5
1.0

Which of the reading is inconsistent?
solution:
For meter bridge:  Rl=X100-l
1→100060=X40⇒X=20003=666.67 Ω
2→10013=X27⇒X=669.23 Ω
3→101.5=X98.5⇒X=656.67 Ω
4→11=X99⇒X=99 Ω
Resistance calculated by using data from 4th observation is most inconsistent.

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge, there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale and the main scale moves by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> on a complete rotation of the circular scale. The zero of circular scale lies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> divisions below the line of graduation when two studs are brought in contact with each other. When a wire is placed between the studs, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> linear scale divisions are clearly visible while 46<sup>th</sup> division the circular scale coincide with the reference line. The diameter of the wire is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: In a screw gauge, there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale and the main scale moves by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> on a complete rotation of the circular scale. The zero of circular scale lies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> divisions below the line of graduation when two studs are brought in contact with each other. When a wire is placed between the studs, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> linear scale divisions are clearly visible while 46<sup>th</sup> division the circular scale coincide with the reference line. The diameter of the wire is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Least count of screw gauge is=PitchNo. of circular divisions

=0.5 mm100

Least count, LC=5×10-3 mm

The zero of circular scale lies 6 divisions below the line of graduation. So, zero error is positive.

Positive zero error =MSR+CSR(LC)

=0 mm+65×10-3 mm

Diameter of wire, d=MSR+CSR (LC)–Positive zero error

=4×0.5 mm+465×10-3-65×10-3 mm

=2 mm+40×5×10-3 mm=2.2 mm

Hence, diameter of wire is 220×10-2 mm.

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge, 5 complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of $0.25$ $\mathrm{cm}$. There are 100 circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of 4 main scale divisions and 30 circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible zero error, the thickness of the wire is:
question: In a screw gauge, 5 complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of $0.25$ $\mathrm{cm}$. There are 100 circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of 4 main scale divisions and 30 circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible zero error, the thickness of the wire is:
solution:
Least count
$$
=\frac{\text { Value of } 1 \text { part on main scale }}{\text { Number of parts on vernier scale }}
$$
$$
=\frac{0.25}{5 \times 100} \mathrm{~cm}=5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}
$$
Reading $=4 \times 0.05 \mathrm{~cm}+30 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}$ $=(0.2+0.0150) \mathrm{cm}=0.2150 \mathrm{~cm}$ (Thickness of wire)

question: Correct set up to verify Ohm's law is :
solution: In ohm's law, we check $V=I R$ where $I$ is the corrent flowing through a resistor and $\mathrm{V}$ is the potential difference across that resistor. Only option (a) fits the above criteria. Remember that ammeter is connected in series with resistance and voltmeter parallel with the resistance.

subject: Physics
q:
A screw gauge of pitch 0.5 mm is used to measure the diameter of uniform wire of length 6.8 cm, the main scale reading is 1.5 mm and circular scale reading is 7. The calculated curved surface area of wire to appropriate significant figures is
[Screw gauge has 50 divisions on the circular scale]
question:
A screw gauge of pitch 0.5 mm is used to measure the diameter of uniform wire of length 6.8 cm, the main scale reading is 1.5 mm and circular scale reading is 7. The calculated curved surface area of wire to appropriate significant figures is
[Screw gauge has 50 divisions on the circular scale]
solution:
Least Count (L.C)=PN=0.5 mm50=0.01 mm

Length of wire =6.8 cm

Diameter of wire =1.5 mm+7× L.C

=1.5 mm+7×.01=1.57 mm

Curved surface area =πDl

=3.14×6.8×1.57×10-1 cm2

=3.352 cm2=3.4 cm2

subject: Physics
q:
Diameter of a steel ball is measured using a Vernier calipers which has divisions of 0.1 cm on its main scale (MS) and 10 divisions of its Vernier scale (VS) match 9 divisions on the main scale. Three such measurements for a ball are given as:
S.No. MS (cm) VS divisions
1. 0.5 8
2. 0.5 4
3. 0.5 6

If the zero error is - 0.03 cm, then mean corrected diameter is:
question:
Diameter of a steel ball is measured using a Vernier calipers which has divisions of 0.1 cm on its main scale (MS) and 10 divisions of its Vernier scale (VS) match 9 divisions on the main scale. Three such measurements for a ball are given as:
S.No. MS (cm) VS divisions
1. 0.5 8
2. 0.5 4
3. 0.5 6

If the zero error is - 0.03 cm, then mean corrected diameter is:
solution:
L.C. of Vernier calipers

= 1 main scale divisionTotal divisons vernier scale  

=0.110=0.01 cm

Reading of Vernier calipers

=M.S.R. +L.C×vs divisions.

d1=0.5+0.01×8

d2=0.5+0.01×4

d3=0.5+0.01×6

∴    Measured diameter are respectively

0.58 cm     0.54 cm,    0.56 cm

∴   average diameter = 0.58 + 0.54 + 0.563 

=1.683=0.56

Negative zero error is added in the average diameter to get the corrected diameter

∴   corrected diameter=0.56-(-0.03)

=0.56+0.03=0.59 cm

subject: Physics
q: A potentiometer wire AB having length L and resistance 12r is joined to a cell D of emf ε and internal resistance r. A cell C having EMF ε/2 and internal resistance 3r is connected. The length AJ, at which the galvanometer, as shown in the figure, shows no deflection is
question: A potentiometer wire AB having length L and resistance 12r is joined to a cell D of emf ε and internal resistance r. A cell C having EMF ε/2 and internal resistance 3r is connected. The length AJ, at which the galvanometer, as shown in the figure, shows no deflection is
solution:
Potential difference across AJ=I RAJ,

=ε13r·12rL·x

For no current in galvanometer,

ε2= potential difference across AJ

ε2=12ε13Lx

x=13L24

subject: Physics
q: In a Vernier calipers, when both jaws touch each other, zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the right of zero of the main scale and $7^{\text{th}}$ Vernier division coincides with a main scale reading. If the value of $1$ main scale division is $1$ mm and there are $10$ Vernier scale divisions, then the Vernier caliper has
question: In a Vernier calipers, when both jaws touch each other, zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the right of zero of the main scale and $7^{\text{th}}$ Vernier division coincides with a main scale reading. If the value of $1$ main scale division is $1$ mm and there are $10$ Vernier scale divisions, then the Vernier caliper has
solution:
Least count of the Vernier calipers is given by $\text{LC} = \dfrac{1 \text{ MSD}}{\text{Number of VSD}}$.

Substituting the given values, $\text{LC} = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{10} = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ cm}$.

Since the zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the right of the zero of the main scale, the zero error is positive.

The zero error is calculated as $+ (n \times \text{LC})$, where $n$ is the coinciding Vernier division.

Given that the $7^{\text{th}}$ Vernier division coincides with a main scale reading, $n = 7$.

Zero error $= + (7 \times 0.01 \text{ cm}) = + 0.07 \text{ cm}$.

Therefore, the Vernier caliper has a $0.07 \text{ cm}$ positive zero error.

Answer: $0.07$ cm positive zero error

subject: Physics
q: The least count of a screw guage is 0.01 mm . If the pitch is increased by $75 \%$ and number of divisions on the circular scale is reduced by $50 \%$, the new least count will be _____ $\times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}$
question: The least count of a screw guage is 0.01 mm . If the pitch is increased by $75 \%$ and number of divisions on the circular scale is reduced by $50 \%$, the new least count will be _____ $\times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Given least count of Screw Gauge }=0.01 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { L.C }=\frac{(\text { pitch })}{\text { No. of circular turn }}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{N}}=0.01 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { New pitch }=\frac{\mathrm{P}(1+0.75)}{\mathrm{N}(1-0.5)}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{N}}\left[\frac{1.75}{0.5}\right] \\ & =(0.01) 3.5 \\ & =0.035 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & =35 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A student in the laboratory measures thickness of a wire using screw gauge. The readings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>19</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The percentage error is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>121</mn></mfrac><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A student in the laboratory measures thickness of a wire using screw gauge. The readings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>19</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The percentage error is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>121</mn></mfrac><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
The average value of the readings is xavg=1.19+1.20+1.22+1.234=1.21

Now, absolute error in measurement Δxavg=0.02+0.01+0.01+0.024=0.064=0.015

Percentage error in measurement is =0.0151.21×100%=150121%

So, the value of x=150.

q: A tunning fork of frequency <math><mn>480</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> is used in an experiment for measuring speed of sound <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> in air by resonance tube method. Resonance is observed to occur at two successive lengths of the air column, <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>70</mn><mtext>  </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, <math><mi>ν</mi></math> is equal to:
question: A tunning fork of frequency <math><mn>480</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> is used in an experiment for measuring speed of sound <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> in air by resonance tube method. Resonance is observed to occur at two successive lengths of the air column, <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>70</mn><mtext>  </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, <math><mi>ν</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Speed of sound, v=2f l2-l1
=2×4800.70-0.30
=384 m/s

subject: Physics
q: A student measuring the diameter of a pencil of circular cross-section with the help of a vernier scale records the following four readings <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>65</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The average of these four reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5375</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the standard deviation of the data is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07395</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The average diameter of the pencil should therefore be recorded as :
question: A student measuring the diameter of a pencil of circular cross-section with the help of a vernier scale records the following four readings <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>55</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>65</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The average of these four reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5375</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the standard deviation of the data is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07395</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. The average diameter of the pencil should therefore be recorded as :
solution:
Significant rule says that reading should has same significant figure as that of reading given.

5.5375→ rounded to →5.54.

0.07395→ rounded to→0.07

therefore, 5.54±0.07 mm

subject: Physics
q: In a meter bridge, the wire of length 1 m has a non-uniform cross-section such that, the variation dRdl of its resistance R with length l is dRdl∝1l. Two equal resistances are connected as shown in the figure. The galvanometer has zero deflection when the jockey is at point P. What is the length AP?
question: In a meter bridge, the wire of length 1 m has a non-uniform cross-section such that, the variation dRdl of its resistance R with length l is dRdl∝1l. Two equal resistances are connected as shown in the figure. The galvanometer has zero deflection when the jockey is at point P. What is the length AP?
solution:
According to question meter bridge have non-uniform wire, where its resistance has a variation with length as dRdl∝1l 

dRdl=kl  ...1

, where k is proportionality constant
Let resistance of the left part AP and right part PB of the wire is RAP , RPB respectively.

From balanced condition of wheat stone bridge is:

PQ=RS

So, from the above condition for given meter bridge is

R'R'=RApRpB  ⇒RAp = RpB  ...2

Now from equation 1, on integrating  ∫dR=k∫dll  ...3

 for AP part of the wire of length extends from o to l from the equation 3

RAP =∫0RAP∫dR=k∫0ldll =k∫0l l-12 dl =k l-12+10l-12+1⇒RAP =2kl

Now, for PB part of the wire of length extends from l to 1 m, from the equation 3

RPB =∫0RPBdR=k∫l1dll =k∫l1 l-12 dl =k l-12+1l1-12+1⇒RPB =2k1-l 

From equation 2 RAP=RPB
∴ 2l=21-l⇒ l=1-l⇒l=12  ⇒l=14=0.25 m

subject: Physics
q:
The vernier scale used for measurement has a positive zero error of 0.2 mm. If while taking a measurement it was noted that 0 on the vernier scale lies between 8.5 cm and 8.6 cm. Vernier coincidence is 6, then the correct value of measurement is cm.

(least count=0.01 cm)
question:
The vernier scale used for measurement has a positive zero error of 0.2 mm. If while taking a measurement it was noted that 0 on the vernier scale lies between 8.5 cm and 8.6 cm. Vernier coincidence is 6, then the correct value of measurement is cm.

(least count=0.01 cm)
solution:
Positive zero error =0.2 mm

Main scale reading =8.5 cm

Vernier scale reading=6×0.01=0.06 cm

Final reading=8.5+0.06-0.02=8.54 cm

subject: Physics
q:
In an experiment to find out the diameter of wire using screw gauge, the following observation were noted:

(a) Screw moves 0.5 mm on main scale in one complete rotation
(b) Total divisions on circular scale =50
(c) Main scale reading is 2.5 mm
(d) 45th  division of circular scale is in the pitch line
(e) Instrument has 0.03 mm negative error Then the diameter of wire is :
question:
In an experiment to find out the diameter of wire using screw gauge, the following observation were noted:

(a) Screw moves 0.5 mm on main scale in one complete rotation
(b) Total divisions on circular scale =50
(c) Main scale reading is 2.5 mm
(d) 45th  division of circular scale is in the pitch line
(e) Instrument has 0.03 mm negative error Then the diameter of wire is :
solution:
From the data given in the question,

Pitch of the screw gauge=0.5 mm.

Total divisions on the circular scalen=50.

Therefore, the least count of the screw gauge is LC=pitchn=0.550=0.01 mm

Now, MSR=2.5 mm, CSR=45

Diameter reading =MSR+LC×CSR- zero error

⇒d=2.5+0.45--0.03

⇒d=2.98 mm

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions on the main scale of a Vernier calliper coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> divisions on the Vernier scale. If each division on the main scale is of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> units, the least count of the instrument is :
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions on the main scale of a Vernier calliper coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> divisions on the Vernier scale. If each division on the main scale is of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> units, the least count of the instrument is :
solution:
Given:

10 MSD=11 VSD

⇒1 VSD=1011 MSD

LC=1MSD-1VSD

=1 MSD-1011 MSD

=1 MSD11

=511 units

subject: Physics
q: While measuring diameter of wire using screw gauge the following readings were noted. Main scale reading is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and circular scale reading is equal to 42 divisions. Pitch of screw gauge is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and it has 100 divisions on circular scale. The diameter of the wire is $\frac{x}{50} \mathrm{~mm}$. The value of $x$ is :
question: While measuring diameter of wire using screw gauge the following readings were noted. Main scale reading is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and circular scale reading is equal to 42 divisions. Pitch of screw gauge is $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ and it has 100 divisions on circular scale. The diameter of the wire is $\frac{x}{50} \mathrm{~mm}$. The value of $x$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{MSR}=1 \mathrm{~mm}, \mathrm{CSR}=42, \text { pitch }=1 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=\frac{\text { pitch }}{\text { No. of CSD }}=\left(\frac{1}{100}\right)=0.01 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { Diameter }=\mathrm{MSR}+\mathrm{LC} \times \mathrm{CSD} \\ & \text { Diameter }=1+(0.01) \times 42 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { Diameter }=1.42 \mathrm{~mm}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{50} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}=71\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> Vernier divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn></math> main scale divisions of a travelling microscope and one smallest reading of main scale is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> the Vernier constant of travelling microscope is:
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> Vernier divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn></math> main scale divisions of a travelling microscope and one smallest reading of main scale is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> the Vernier constant of travelling microscope is:
solution:
The number of divisions on the vernier scale =50

Main scale division (MSD)=0.5 mm

As, 50 divisions on the vernier scale coincide with 49 divisions on the main scale,

VSD=4950MSD

The minimum inaccuracy in the measurement of distance by a vernier scale,

= Least count of the vernier scale

=1 MSD–1 VSD

=1 MSD-4950MSD

=150MSD=150×0.5 mm

=0.01 mm.

subject: Physics
q: There are 100 divisions on the circular scale of a screw gauge of pitch $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. With no measuring quantity in between the jaws, the zero of the circular scale lies 5 divisions below the reference line. The diameter of a wire is then measured using this screw gauge. It is found that 4 linear scale divisions are clearly visible while 60 divisions on circular scale coincide with the reference line. The diameter of the wire is :
question: There are 100 divisions on the circular scale of a screw gauge of pitch $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. With no measuring quantity in between the jaws, the zero of the circular scale lies 5 divisions below the reference line. The diameter of a wire is then measured using this screw gauge. It is found that 4 linear scale divisions are clearly visible while 60 divisions on circular scale coincide with the reference line. The diameter of the wire is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Least count }=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{~mm}=0.01 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { zero error }=+0.05 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { Reading }=4 \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}+60 \times 0.01 \mathrm{~mm}-0.05 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & =4.55 \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In a vernier calliper, when both jaws touch each other, zero of the vernier scale shifts towards left and its $4^{\text {th }}$ division coincides exactly with a certain division on main scale. If 50 vernier scale divisions equal to 49 main scale divisions and zero error in the instrument is $0.04 \mathrm{~mm}$ then how many main scale divisions are there in $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ ?
question: In a vernier calliper, when both jaws touch each other, zero of the vernier scale shifts towards left and its $4^{\text {th }}$ division coincides exactly with a certain division on main scale. If 50 vernier scale divisions equal to 49 main scale divisions and zero error in the instrument is $0.04 \mathrm{~mm}$ then how many main scale divisions are there in $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ ?
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& 50 \mathrm{VSD}=49 \mathrm{MSD} \\
& 1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{49}{50} \mathrm{MSD} \\
& \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD} \\
& \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD} \cdot \frac{49}{50} \mathrm{MSD}=\frac{\mathrm{MSD}}{50} \ldots . . . . . .(1) \\
& \text {Now, } | \text { zero error }|=| 0-4(\mathrm{~L} . \mathrm{C}) | \\
& 0.04=|-4(\mathrm{~L} . \mathrm{C})| \Rightarrow \mathrm{L} . \mathrm{C}=0.01 \mathrm{~mm} \\
& \mathrm{0 . 0 4}=|-\mathrm{4}(\mathrm{L} . \mathrm{C})| \Rightarrow \mathrm{L} . \mathrm{C} \ldots . . .(2) \\
& =\mathrm{0 . 0 1} \mathrm{mm}
\end{aligned}$
Using (1) and (2)
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{M S D}{50}=0.01 \\
& M S D=0.5 \mathrm{~mm} \\
& M S D=\frac{1 \mathrm{~cm}}{\mathrm{~N}} \\
& \mathrm{~N}=\frac{1 \mathrm{~cm}}{0.5 \mathrm{~mm}}=\frac{100 \mathrm{~mm}}{5 \mathrm{~mm}} \\
& \mathrm{~N}=20
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A load of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is suspended from a steel wire of length <math><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> and radius <math><mn>1.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi></math> in Searle's apparatus experiment. The increase in length produced in the wire is <math><mn>4.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Now the load is fully immersed in a liquid of relative density <math><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The relative density of the material of load is <math><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The new value of increase in length of the steel wire is:
question: A load of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is suspended from a steel wire of length <math><mn>2</mn><mi>m</mi></math> and radius <math><mn>1.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi></math> in Searle's apparatus experiment. The increase in length produced in the wire is <math><mn>4.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Now the load is fully immersed in a liquid of relative density <math><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The relative density of the material of load is <math><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The new value of increase in length of the steel wire is:
solution:
δ=ρ0vg×LAy

δ′=ρ0-ρLvg×LAy⇒δ^′δ=ρ0-ρLρ0=8-28

δ′=3 mm

subject: Physics
q: The pitch of the screw gauge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale. When nothing is put in between the jaws, the zero of the circular scale lies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> divisions below the reference line. When a wire is placed between the jaws, the first linear scale division is clearly visible while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>72</mn><mtext>nd </mtext></msup></math> division on circular scale coincides with the reference line. The radius of the wire is
question: The pitch of the screw gauge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale. When nothing is put in between the jaws, the zero of the circular scale lies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> divisions below the reference line. When a wire is placed between the jaws, the first linear scale division is clearly visible while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>72</mn><mtext>nd </mtext></msup></math> division on circular scale coincides with the reference line. The radius of the wire is
solution:
Least count =1 mm100=0.01 mm

zero error =+8×LC=+0.08 mm

True reading (Diameter)

=1 mm+72×LC- (Zero error)

=1 mm+72×0.01 mm-0.08 mm

=1.72 mm-0.08 mm

=1.64 mm

therefore, radius =1.642=0.82 mm.

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment with vernier callipers of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, when two jaws are joined together the zero of vernier scale lies right to the zero of the main scale and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of vernier scale coincides with the main scale division. While measuring the diameter of a spherical bob, the zero of vernier scale lies in between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> marks and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of vernier scale coincides with the main scale division. The diameter of bob is measured as
question: In an experiment with vernier callipers of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>, when two jaws are joined together the zero of vernier scale lies right to the zero of the main scale and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of vernier scale coincides with the main scale division. While measuring the diameter of a spherical bob, the zero of vernier scale lies in between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> marks and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of vernier scale coincides with the main scale division. The diameter of bob is measured as
solution:
The least count is LC=0.1 mm

Zero error (ZE)=6×LC=6×0.1 mm=0.6 mm

The total reading is TR=MSR+VSR×LC-ZE⇒TR=32 mm+(0.1×4) mm-0.6 mm⇒TR=31.8 mm=3.18 cm

subject: Physics
q: $N$ divisions on the main scale of a vernier calliper coincide with $(N+1)$ divisions of the vernier scale. If each division of main scale is ' $a$ ' units, then the least count of the instrument is
question: $N$ divisions on the main scale of a vernier calliper coincide with $(N+1)$ divisions of the vernier scale. If each division of main scale is ' $a$ ' units, then the least count of the instrument is
solution:
No of divisions on main scale $=N$
No of divisions on vernier scale $=N+1$ size of main scale division $=a$
Let size of vernier scale division be $b$ then we have
$$
a N=b(N+1) \Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=\frac{a N}{N+1}
$$
Least count is $\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{a}-\frac{a N}{N+1}$ $=a\left[\frac{N+1-N}{N+1}\right]=\frac{a}{N+1}$

subject: Physics
q: A vernier callipers has 20 divisions on the vernier scale, which coincides with $19^{\text {th }}$ division on the main scale. The least count of the instrument is $0.1 \mathrm{~mm}$. One main scale division is equal to _____$\mathrm{mm}$.
question: A vernier callipers has 20 divisions on the vernier scale, which coincides with $19^{\text {th }}$ division on the main scale. The least count of the instrument is $0.1 \mathrm{~mm}$. One main scale division is equal to _____$\mathrm{mm}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 20 \mathrm{VSD}=19 \mathrm{MSD} \\ & 1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{19}{20} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & \text { L.C. }=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD} \\ & 0.1 \mathrm{~mm}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-\frac{19}{20} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & 0.1=\frac{1}{20} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & 1 \mathrm{MSD}=2 \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Student A and student B used two screw gauges of equal pitch and 100 equal circular divisions to measure the radius of a given wire. The actual value of the radius of the wire is 0.322 cm. The absolute value of the difference between the final circular scale readings observed by the students A and B is _______.

[Figure shows position of reference O when jaws of screw gauge are closed]

Given pitch =0.1 cm.
question:
Student A and student B used two screw gauges of equal pitch and 100 equal circular divisions to measure the radius of a given wire. The actual value of the radius of the wire is 0.322 cm. The absolute value of the difference between the final circular scale readings observed by the students A and B is _______.

[Figure shows position of reference O when jaws of screw gauge are closed]

Given pitch =0.1 cm.
solution:
For A,

Reading =MSR+CSR+Error

0.322=0.300+CSR+5×LC

0.322=0.300+CSR+0.005

CSR=0.017

For B, 

Reading =MSR+CSR+Error

0.322=0.200+CSR+0.092

CSR=0.030

Difference =0.030-0.017=0.013 cm

Division on circular scale =0.0130.001=13

q: The diameter of a spherical bob is measured using a vernier callipers. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions of the main scale, in the vernier callipers, are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of vernier scale. One main scale division is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>8</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of vernier scale was found to coincide exactly with one of the main scale division. If the given vernier callipers has positive zero error of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the radius of the bob is _____________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: The diameter of a spherical bob is measured using a vernier callipers. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions of the main scale, in the vernier callipers, are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of vernier scale. One main scale division is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>8</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of vernier scale was found to coincide exactly with one of the main scale division. If the given vernier callipers has positive zero error of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>04</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the radius of the bob is _____________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
9 MSD=10 VSD
1 VSD=0.9 mm
1 MSD=1 mm
∴ L.C.=MSD-VSDMSD=1-0.91=0.1
Reading = Main scale reading + L.C. × vernier scale reading
=10+0.1×8
=10.8 mm

Reading of diameter=10.8 mm-0.4 mm=10.4 mm
radius of the bob=5.2 mm
=0.52 cm=52×10-2 cm

subject: Physics
q: The measured value of the length of a simple pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> accuracy. The time for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> oscillations was measured to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn></math> seconds with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> second resolution. From these measurements, the accuracy in the measurement of acceleration due to gravity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> is:
question: The measured value of the length of a simple pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> accuracy. The time for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> oscillations was measured to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn></math> seconds with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> second resolution. From these measurements, the accuracy in the measurement of acceleration due to gravity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi><mo>%</mo></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> is:
solution:
Time period of a simple pendulum is given by, T=2πlg.

⇒g=4π2lT2

Therefore, fractional error will be

Δgg=Δll+2ΔTT

=0.220+2140

=1.220

Percentage change =1.220×100=6%

subject: Physics
q: A tiny metallic rectangular sheet has length and breadth of 5 mm and 2.5 mm , respectively. Using a specially designed screw gauge which has pitch of 0.75 mm and 15 divisions in the circular scale, you are asked to find the area of the sheet. In this measurement, the maximum fractional error will be $\frac{x}{100}$ where $x$ is _________.
question: A tiny metallic rectangular sheet has length and breadth of 5 mm and 2.5 mm , respectively. Using a specially designed screw gauge which has pitch of 0.75 mm and 15 divisions in the circular scale, you are asked to find the area of the sheet. In this measurement, the maximum fractional error will be $\frac{x}{100}$ where $x$ is _________.
solution: Since least count of the instrument can be calculated as $\begin{aligned} & \text { Least count }=\frac{\text { pitch length }}{\text { No. of division on circular scale }} \\ & =\frac{0.75}{15}=0.05 \mathrm{~mm} . \end{aligned}$ Here we are provided $\mathrm{L}=5 \mathrm{~mm} \& \mathrm{~W}=2.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ $\mathrm{L}=5 \mathrm{~mm} \& \mathrm{~W}=2.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ $\because$ We know that $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{L} . \mathrm{W}$ For calculating fractional error, we can write $\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{\mathrm{dL}}{\mathrm{~L}}+\frac{\mathrm{dW}}{\mathrm{~W}}$ Here $\mathrm{dL}=\mathrm{dW}=0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{0.05}{5}+\frac{0.05}{2.5} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{~A}}=\frac{1}{100}+\frac{2}{100}=\frac{3}{100} \end{aligned}$ So, $x=3$

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge when the circular scale is given five complete rotations it moves linearly by $2.5$ mm. If the circular scale has $100$ divisions, the least count of screw gauge is _____ mm.
question: In a screw gauge when the circular scale is given five complete rotations it moves linearly by $2.5$ mm. If the circular scale has $100$ divisions, the least count of screw gauge is _____ mm.
solution:
Pitch of the screw gauge is the linear distance moved in one complete rotation.

$\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{2.5}{5} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$

Least count of the screw gauge is given by the ratio of the pitch to the total number of divisions on the circular scale.

$\text{Least count} = \dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Number of divisions on circular scale}}$

$\text{Least count} = \dfrac{0.5}{100} = 0.005 \text{ mm}$

$\text{Least count} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$

Answer: $5 \times 10^{-3}$

subject: Physics
q: The resistance of the meter bridge AB in given figure is $4 \Omega$. With a cell of emf $\varepsilon=0.5 \mathrm{~V}$ and rheostat resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=2 \Omega$ the null point is obtained at some point J. When the cell is replaced by another one of emf $\varepsilon=\varepsilon_{2}$ the same null point $\mathrm{J}$ is found for $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=6 \Omega$. The $\operatorname{emf} \varepsilon_{2}$ is:
question: The resistance of the meter bridge AB in given figure is $4 \Omega$. With a cell of emf $\varepsilon=0.5 \mathrm{~V}$ and rheostat resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=2 \Omega$ the null point is obtained at some point J. When the cell is replaced by another one of emf $\varepsilon=\varepsilon_{2}$ the same null point $\mathrm{J}$ is found for $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=6 \Omega$. The $\operatorname{emf} \varepsilon_{2}$ is:
solution:
Given, Emf of cell, $\varepsilon=0.5 \mathrm{v}$ Rheostat resistance, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=2 \Omega$ Potential gradient is

$\frac{d v}{d L}=\left(\frac{6}{2+4}\right) \times \frac{4}{L}$

Let null point be at $\ell \mathrm{cm}$ when cell of $\mathrm{emf} \varepsilon=0.5 \mathrm{v}$ is used.

thus $\varepsilon_{1}=0.5 \mathrm{~V}=\left(\frac{6}{2+4}\right) \times \frac{4}{\mathrm{~L}} \times \ell$...(i)

For resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{h}}=6 \Omega$ new potential gradient is $\left(\frac{6}{4+6}\right) \times \frac{4}{L}$ and at null point

$\left(\frac{6}{4+6}\right)\left(\frac{4}{\mathrm{~L}}\right) \times \ell=\varepsilon_{2}$...(ii)

Dividing equation (i) by (ii) we get

$\frac{0.5}{\varepsilon_{2}}=\frac{10}{6}$ thus $\varepsilon_{2}=0.3 \mathrm{v}$

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to determine the gravitational acceleration g of a place with the help of a simple pendulum, the measured time period squared is plotted against the string length of the pendulum in the figure. What is the value of g at the place?
question: In an experiment to determine the gravitational acceleration g of a place with the help of a simple pendulum, the measured time period squared is plotted against the string length of the pendulum in the figure. What is the value of g at the place?
solution:
T=2πLg

⇒   g=4π2LT2

T2L=4π2g=tanθ=slope of graph

⇒   g=4π2tanθ

∵  tanθ=8−22−0.5=61.5=4

⇒   g=4π24=(3.14)2=π2=3.142=9.87 m s-2

subject: Physics
q: Using screw gauge of pitch <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions on its circular scale, the thickness of an object is measured. It should correctly be recorded as,
question: Using screw gauge of pitch <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions on its circular scale, the thickness of an object is measured. It should correctly be recorded as,
solution:
Thickness = M.S. Reading + Circular Scale Reading (L.C.)

Here least count is 0.002 cm per division.

From all the four options, the second answer is divisible by this least count. Hence option 2 is correct.

subject: Physics
q: A screw gauge with a pitch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.5 </mn><mi>mm</mi></math> and a circular scale with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions is used to measure the thickness of a thin sheet of aluminium. Before starting the measurement, it is found that when the two jaws of the screw gauge are brought in contact, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></msup></math> division coincides with the main scale line and that the zero of the main scale is barely visible. What is the thickness of the sheet if the main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.5 </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>25</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></msup></math> division coincides with the main scale line?
question: A screw gauge with a pitch of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.5 </mn><mi>mm</mi></math> and a circular scale with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions is used to measure the thickness of a thin sheet of aluminium. Before starting the measurement, it is found that when the two jaws of the screw gauge are brought in contact, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></msup></math> division coincides with the main scale line and that the zero of the main scale is barely visible. What is the thickness of the sheet if the main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.5 </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>25</mn><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mrow></msup></math> division coincides with the main scale line?
solution:
LC=0.550=0.01 mm

Zero error =0.50-0.45=-0.05

Thickness =0.5+25×0.01+0.05

=0.50+0.25+0.05

=0.80 mm
